entry.title: Dropping scheduled documents from Webi to SharePoint on line

Last year, MS depreciated the API allowing scheduled WebI documents to be dropped directly to SharePoint On line folders.  Has anyone found a manageable work around for this feature.

entry.title: Transform Your BW Modernization Journey: Join SAP’s 3-Episode Modernization Masterclass
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud: Best Practices for BW Modernization Miniseries 

This comprehensive training series empowers SAP Partners with practical approaches and proven methodologies to successfully modernize BW environments. Whether you’re dealing with Private Cloud Edition transitions, greenfield implementations, or advanced data product strategies, this series covers it all.

Episode 1: Readiness and Planning (May 25)

Master the fundamentals of Private Cloud Edition (PCE) transitions. You’ll explore:

  • Business drivers and assessment methodologies
  • Sizing best practices and Data Volume Management techniques
  • Project roadmapping and interface preservation strategies
  • Real customer examples and risk mitigation approaches

Episode 2: Greenfield/Brownfield with SAP Datasphere (May 26)

Dive deep into cloud-native analytics platform design using Datasphere:

  • Advanced modeling patterns and semantic layers
  • Space management and cloud workload sizing
  • Implementation approaches and governance frameworks
  • Enterprise-scale data control authorization concepts

Episode 3: Data Product Generator (DPG) Best Practices (May 27)

Master the art of creating extendable, reusable data products:

  • Extension patterns and historical data migration strategies
  • Mixed-model scenarios integrating DPG with Datasphere and legacy BW
  • Hands-on Technical Academy demonstrations
  • Comparative analysis of different modernization approaches

Ready to elevate your BW modernization expertise? Register now for this foundational learning series and position yourself as the go-to partner for successful analytics transformations. SAP BDC: Best Practices for BW Modernizatio

May 25

SAP BDC: Best Practices for BW Modernization miniseries- Episode 1: Introduction

May 26

SAP BDC: Best Practices for BW Modernization miniseries- Episode 2: Greenfield Datasphere Approach

May 27

SAP BDC: Best Practices for BW Modernization miniseries- Episode 3: Data Product Generator

 

Earn Your Digital Badge 🏆 

Partners who complete all three episodes will receive a digital badge recognizing their expertise in BW modernization - a valuable credential to showcase your specialized knowledge to customers and prospects.

entry.title: A conversation with Takvor Tilkiyan, an SAPUI5 Application Development Enthusiast
entry.content:
This blog post is an informal conversation between a student and a teacher. The student wants to understand what has sparked his teacher's drive to learn and use the SAPUI5 framework as a hobby.  In a technology landscape dominated by AI's efficiency, human stories remain one of the great tools to ignite passion for learning, creativity, and optimism. Our hope is that this conversation will help readers learn more about the SAPUI5 framework while getting to know Takvor and his journey as an enthusiast of the SAPUI5 community.

Hello Takvor, in 2025, I attended your "SAPUI5 Application Development" one-day training session and was delighted to be part of it. I understand that you organize and deliver these trainings based on your experience with SAPUI5 and a genuine passion for this technology. Could you share your background and how you got started with SAPUI5 as a hobby? What led you to begin using it?

Takvor: I initially started using SAPUI5 as part of my tasks on one of our SAP projects. However, this was in the early days of SAPUI5 when there were no mobile controls yet. After about a year and a half of working with the framework, I applied for a one-month fellowship within SAP. During this fellowship, I helped SAPUI5 colleagues develop applications using the freshly released SAP m mobile controls.

This time required me to deepen my knowledge of SAPUI5, which truly ignited my enthusiasm for the framework. I realized that using a unified framework like SAPUI5 allows the development of applications accessible from various devices and hosted on a web server. This means that SAPUI5 applications are easy to maintain and accessible to all users and devices. This discovery ignited my interest in further developing my knowledge and diving deeper into the framework.

I read all available SAPUI5 documentation, developer guides, and related topics. I was eager to put this knowledge into practice, and the following year, there was a competition to develop an application that had to be rendered on a mobile device. So, I decided to develop the UI for such an application using SAPUI5 m controls to practice the knowledge I had gained. Initially, I focused on developing the user interface and participating in the competition.

The application itself was interesting; it was about sharing information on who drives what car in the company (Carfleet). So, if you wanted to buy a car, you could find someone who owned that model and seek advice.

Next, I further developed this application by adding a back end. Eventually, this application became generally available to all colleagues within SAP. At the same time, the UI was developed in accordance with all SAPUI5 documentation standards, so I used it as a basis for the training I started. I wanted to share the SAPUI5 knowledge I had gained with other colleagues, so I launched the training program.

That's how it all came together— I developed the training using my experience developing this application, meaning the application serves as a practical example of how best practices are applied. That's my background related to SAPUI5.

 

What challenges did you face while learning SAPUI5 as a framework, and how did you overcome them? I find this question particularly interesting because, as I understand it, you've been learning by doing—essentially self-taught and figuring things out on your own.

So, when considering overcoming these challenges, I believe it's important for young learners to understand not just the external obstacles, but also to understand their own internal motivation. Apart from the challenges, what led to your success? Was it curiosity, persistence, or other personal traits? If you could name some qualities that helped you succeed, that would be great!

Takvor: Yeah, sure! My main job at SAP isn't related to UI technologies at all. Throughout the projects I've participated in, I've had some UI-related tasks after the initial project, but maybe 80% or 90% of my work is back-end. Right now, I'm working with Golang on the Signify service. This is a Hyperspace service used to digitally sign and verify artifacts at SAP. We don't have a graphical user interface—it's all API-based.

I'm saying this to highlight that my experience with SAPUI5 is entirely hobby-based. It's serious, yet one of my passions and interests that I want to keep alive, even as new developments emerge.

Regarding time spans, I didn't have deadlines for developing my SAPUI5 applications or expanding the training course content. I was doing it at my own pace, reading all new topics in the SAPUI5 documentation. That's probably one reason that helped me learn—there was no pressure, which usually isn't the case. However, if someone wants to learn SAPUI5, there's plenty to learn and various ways to do so.

The documentation—the Demo Kit—is filled with guidelines and examples. Another great aspect of UI technology is that you get immediate, interactive examples you can inspect and directly interact with. There are samples within the Demo Kit where you can modify the code—or copy, paste, change, and start using it immediately.

There is a wealth of information about SAPUI5, and with time and interest, you can explore and understand it. The topics are complex because the framework is comprehensive, covering everything needed for enterprise applications. The toolset and utilities are all there. The good thing about the framework is that it's very well structured. While it might be complex, the topics are logically connected and make sense. Once you learn the fundamentals, you'll be able to build your tasks or UIs as you wish.

Compared to other frameworks, can you give an example for those interested in learning SAPUI5? How can we illustrate this complexity, perhaps in relation to another technology? Is there an example we can provide to help them understand?

Takvor: Sure. One example is the sheer number of formats and languages you need to know while building an SAPUI5 application. You're primarily working with JavaScript, but you also need to understand XML for views. You should know HTML here and there—not extensively, but somewhat. CSS is also necessary. YAML is another important format, mainly for setting up your development environment, builds, and local servers. JSON is used in many areas—not directly everywhere, but still important to be familiar with. These formats contribute to the complexity.

Another aspect is the baseline artifacts required to build even a simple but standard application. You can create a basic application with just one HTML file—that's fine—but in practice, that's not how most applications are built. Most are structured as components with component descriptors, views, a root view, etc.

For example, if you want to create a standard application with simple functionality like displaying "Hello World"—just to show something on the UI—you first create the index.html. You'll then need a Component.js, which describes your component because applications are either embedded in LaunchPads or run as independent units. Next, you'll need a manifest.json to configure this component and set up routes to the application. In the manifest.json, you specify your root view, which is like a container for all other views. Then, in this root view, you start laying out different pages represented by views and controllers.

That structure alone is complex, and the multitude of layers may sound intimidating. On each level, different formats and configurations exist. For example, in manifest.json, you should learn how routes are configured. In the root view, you should know your root container. In Component.js, it's often straightforward—mostly copy-pasting—but if you initialize some models in Component.js, you place them there. Index.html might be the simplest of all these levels, but even then, you need a well-configured script to bootstrap the application.

All this contributes to the complexity, which might confuse many colleagues. Yes, it's rewarding when you connect all these details and observe the results, such as applications for the car fleet or skills transfer across the organization. A major advantage of SAPUI5 is its foundation on open standards like JavaScript, HTML, and CSS. Most of these technologies are open standards, making the framework technology-agnostic. Even if you require something specific—like an external library or UI controls—you can easily integrate these libraries into the standard framework as they're built on open standards.

You mentioned three levels of reusability. Could you describe the three levels of reusability and the standardization you mentioned in SAPUI5?

Takvor: Yeah, there are three approaches to consuming SAPUI5. At SAP, we aim for all applications to have a consistent look and feel. This is the basic goal—the minimum requirement is that your application uses standard themes and has a uniform appearance.

First Level of Reusability: This is Fiori Elements. If you want just this, you can use Fiori Elements. I think they're based on web components. I've not used them in my apps—I'm using the SAP controls directly provided by the framework. But if your team consists of developers familiar with Angular or React and insists on using them, you can still build your UI in those frameworks while achieving the standard SAP look and feel. This is the first way to consume SAPUI5, the first level of reusability.

Second Level of Reusability: This is the full SAPUI5 Framework. On the other hand, the SAPUI5 framework is a comprehensive UI framework. Using only it, you can build a full-fledged enterprise application featuring a standard look, controls standard to UI5 developers, Model-View-Controller architecture, JSON models, other data models, and internationalization—essentially all major features needed in a UI application are provided. I employ this approach in my two applications. It's intended for standard UI developers who want all features SAPUI5 provides.

Third Level of Reusability: These are the Smart Controls. The third type of reusability involves Smart Controls. This approach is for developers with little or no UI experience. For example, ABAP developers who have decades of back-end experience but have never worked on UI development can use a limited set of SAPUI5 controls called Dynamic Controls, configuring appearance, behavior, and interaction through metadata. They build their backend or data service as preferred, using instructions or annotations interpreted by Smart Controls—UI controls that deliver intended functionality. This approach is often used when building something standardized, with no deviations in look, feel, and interaction, relying mostly on back-end logic.

To summarize:

  • The first approach targets UI developers experienced in popular frameworks but less familiar with the SAPUI5 complexity.
  • The second level is intended for standard UI developers seeking the full power of SAPUI5.
  • The third level is designed for back-end developers requiring minimal front-end development for UI creation.

Are there any controls or parts of the technology that are favorites of yours? Do you have controls that you particularly enjoy using? Are there controls you believe could be improved? What are your thoughts?

Takvor: Yeah, one of the most useful controls is the FlexBox controls in SAPUI5, based on the popular CSS Flexible Box Layout standard. They include HBox (horizontal box) and VBox (vertical box), serving as highly configurable and responsive areas or containers. When you shrink your browser or open the app on a mobile device, the controls inside automatically rearrange. With FlexBox, you can easily build your layout.

For example, if I need cards or forms—like when dealing with a dialog containing a form—I use FlexBox as it's simple and flexible.

Regarding not just controls, but also the eventing system—the Event Bus, specifically—is very useful. I aim to keep the different views and components of the app independent to avoid hard references or API ties. This is achieved when having a dialog with a view and controller, and a view showing this dialog. To keep the dialog and logic separate from the parent view, their connection is easily managed by the Event Bus.

For example, if you wish to pass data or trigger an event from the dialog to the view and then process it, you simply send it as an Event Bus event. This separates UI components, making the application cleaner and more organized.

Since you conduct SAPUI5 training sessions, what is your impression of how participants respond to the technology? Is there something you notice people particularly like, or something that challenges them? 

Takvor: I think the most challenging part for colleagues in my training courses is SAPUI5 bindings. We spend significant time explaining binding methods—the various syntax options for achieving the same or complex bindings. This topic engages them greatly, and it's where they often make mistakes.

I've observed that colleagues feel happy when they understand connections. Once they grasp what ties together and how things connect, they gain control over the technology. As mentioned earlier, the framework is complex but logical and well-structured. With enough time and attention, one can build a mental model of the application. Upon mastering this model and gaining experience—for example, knowing available controls, tools, and events—you start working with confidence.

In our preparation for this conversation, we discussed the UI5 community and support network, noting pros and cons. How does the SAPUI5 framework compete with other, likely larger communities? As an enterprise framework, what advantages does SAPUI5 and its community offer?

Takvor: If you aim to build something within the SAP environment, SAPUI5 is a great choice as it's the standard framework. Although it's more complex than React or Angular, most SAP applications are built with it. Internally at SAP, examples and support abound.

The framework is not only well-structured but also based on open standards. Suppose you encounter difficulties with an aspect—FlexBox, for instance—if documentation lacks information, you can explore open standards, like standard CSS Flexible Box controls, for online support.

Nearly all SAPUI5 controls are based on widely-used open standards, providing opportunities to understand and extend them as needed. The level of standardization in SAPUI5 technologies is very high.

How often do you offer the SAPUI5 Application Development training, and how can people join if interested?

Takvor: We plan to offer the "SAPUI5 Application Development" training twice a year, likely mid-year and in the second half. Colleagues can follow the newsletter in the section "Trainings in Focus." 

The approach is practical and hands-on—though not wholly hands-on—but focuses on diving into real application code and learning concepts. That's the training style.

entry.title: Data migration strategy – SF Recruiting to SmartRecruiters (transition scenarios vs greenfield)
entry.content:
Hi SAP community,
 
I’m currently working on a SmartRecruiters implementation in a SAP SuccessFactors context, for a customer already using SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting (transition scenario, not greenfield).
 
So far, most of my experience has been on greenfield implementations, where the recommended approach is quite clear:
- limit data migration as much as possible
- focus on migrating only active jobs and candidates
- treat full historical migration as an exception

 

However, in transition projects (SF Recruiting → SmartRecruiters), I’m seeing different expectations from customers.
In particular:
- customers often expect a more comprehensive migration since they remain within the SAP ecosystem
- there is a perception that historical data should be carried over “by default”
 
I would really appreciate feedback from partners who have already delivered this type of project.
Some key questions:
 
1) Migration scope in transition projects 
- Do you typically include active jobs and candidates as part of the standard implementation? 
- How do you position historical data (archived candidates, past applications)? 
 
2) Effort estimation 
- How do you estimate the effort for “active data migration”? 
- Is it included in your base implementation scope or treated as a separate workstream? 
 
3) Customer positioning 
- What messaging do you use to align customer expectations (especially when they expect full migration)? 
 
4) Historical data strategy 
- If you do not migrate historical data:
  - Do customers keep SF Recruiting as a reference system? 
  - For how long is this typically maintained? 
  - Do you provide data export strategies (e.g. CSV, archiving, reporting extracts)? 
 
5) Future tooling / acceleration 
- Are you aware of any tools or initiatives (e.g. Migration Hub) that aim to simplify or standardize transition scenarios? 
- Do you expect these tools to support historical data migration, or mainly configuration and data analysis? 
 
The goal is to align with SAP / SmartRecruiters best practices while managing real customer expectations in transition scenarios.
 
Thanks in advance for your insights and feedback!
Barbara

entry.title: Re: Error on Approval of Service Entry Sheet for Purch Org.
entry.content:
Good day @Rhoida_Macawile 

Thank you for your question.

Question: What can I do if I encounter this error again in the certification assessment which does not allow any configurations? Thank you.

Answer: Since the assessment system is preconfigured and you cannot change roles, you should not try to adjust configuration, roles, or purchasing organisation settings.

Here is what I think you should do:

  1. Take a screenshot of the error popup.
  2. Keep the SES number, purchase order number, purchasing organisation, and workflow item visible where possible.
  3. Open the task again from My Inbox and retry once only.                                                                                                          
  4. If you get the same errors, report it immediately to the exam proctor or assessment support.
  5. Explain to him/her that this is an authorisation issue, not an incorrect process step.
  6. Provide the exact error message you have received.
  7. Continue with the remaining assessment tasks if the system allows you to proceed.

Note that the required correction is an authorisation correction, not and end user process correction. In a restricted assessment environment, only the assessment system administrator or certification support can correct the missing role or catalog assignment. See KBA 2789506 for more details.

In a normal Cloud tenant, the administrator would check the approver’s business role in Maintain Business Roles and ensure the user has the required SES approval authorisation.

KBA 2789506 describes that the approver needs authorisations such as:

  • M_SES_EKO with activity 37 Accepts for the relevant purchasing organisation
  • M_SES_EKG with activity 37 Accept for the relevant purchasing group
  • M_SES_WRK with activity 37 Accept for the relevant plant
  • Good movement related authorisation for movement types 101 and 102

It also states that the issue can be resolved by assigning SAP_BR_PROJ_MANAGE_COMM or adding business catalog SAP_MM_BC_SES_WIAPPROVE_PC to an existing role.

Your current role BR_PROJECT_MANAGER_PROF may allow project management activities, but the error shows that it does not provide the required backend acceptance authorisation for purchasing organisation 1010 in this assessment tenant. The missing authorisation is therefore more specific than general project manager access.

See the following reference for more details.

3060625 - Authorization error when approving Service Entry Sheet - SAP Fiori, SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA. - SAP for Me

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Unable to Post TRM Flows Due to Missing Payment Block...
entry.content:
Dear @Wolfgang1308,

Based on the provided screenshots the error is caused by the combination of Posting = to customer and Payment Request = With for the same Treasury flow. Message T0925 states that the system is configured to generate a payment request in addition to a customer line item. In this case SAP expects a payment blocking indicator on the customer line item to prevent both the payment request and the customer open item from becoming payment-relevant

In Public Cloud the on-premise IMG activity shown in the screenshot Define Payment Blocking Indicators for Accounting Documents is not exposed as an equivalent customer-maintainable SSCUI. The available Public Cloud configuration Check Payment Block Reason SSCUI 101040 is used to maintain/check payment block reasons but it does not provide the same Treasury payment request assignment logic as the on-premise configuration

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3069902

For the Public Cloud Treasury setup, SAP’s documented approach when the Treasury transaction should post to the Business Partner/customer account is to maintain the relevant payment line with:

  • Posting: to customer
  • Payment Request: Without

SAP documents this approach for transactions where the posting should go to the Business Partner account instead of the payment request clearing account

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3676109

Therefore the recommended correction is to avoid the mixed setup:

  1. If the business wants the flow to post to the customer / Business Partner account, keep Posting = to customer but set Payment Request = Without
  2. If the business wants to use the Treasury payment request process the same flow should not also create a customer-payment-relevant open item. In that case the process should follow the payment request route instead

If the transaction is already settled and the fields are locked the correction should be tested before posting the flows, either by adjusting the payment details in the appropriate transaction processing step or by recreating/reversing the test scenario where required

A substitution workaround is not suitable here. SAP confirms that the Payment Block field is not included in the standard list of substitution fields in Manage Substitution / Validation Rules in Public Cloud so it cannot be populated reliably through a standard FI substitution rule

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3682470

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: It's renaming time! (it's a tradition 😂). Introducing SAP Business AI Platform (=BTP, BDC, BTM+)
entry.content:
What is the SAP Business AI Platform? It’s designed as your enterprise AI foundation, bringing together SAP’s AI, data, process, and governance capabilities under a single platform. It provides end-to-end deep process context, unified business data, and enterprise governance — so the agents and applications you build understand your business, not just your prompts.

SAP Business AI Platform is the unified platform that brings together lots of existing technologies SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud, and some capabilities from the SAP Business Transformation Management portfolio. So this includes things like the AI Foundation, the generative AI hub, SAP Knowledge Graph, Intelligent content and domain models, and SAP’s RPT AI model.

And over time, the platform will presumably also incorporate the technology from the recently announced SAP acquisitions such as Reltio for master data management, Dremio, an intelligent data lakehouse platform, and Prior Labs, a pioneer of Tabular Foundation Models for the kinds of structured data that is used to run businesses.

SAP Business AI Platform enables every team, from developers to business users, to build and deploy agentic AI with the process knowledge, business data, and governance built in, so AI delivers trusted results across the enterprise, not just in isolated use cases.

And not that the commercial models remain unchanged at this time — customers can continue using the existing commercial models for current offerings such as SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP AI Core. For newer capabilities, detailed commercial information will be shared when these products and services become generally available

entry.title: Re: Can't add Posting Date from Sales Quote to a report
entry.content:
Hello Benjamin,

The correct field to display the Posting Date of the Sales Quote in the Data Source CRMCQTHB (Sales Quote Header) is the field Calendar Day

For example, if a user with the Time Zone maintained as CET creates a Sales Quote with a Posting Date of the 16.05.2026 this is converted in the backend to UTC and stored as 15.05.2026 22:00:00.00.

When you then add the field Calendar Day to the Data Source CRMCQTHB (Sales Quote Header), the Date is displayed in UTC as the 15.05.2026.

This applies to all Dates in Reports. See also KBA 1954923 - Discrepancy Between Report and Application Data Regarding Time Values - ByD

With kind regards,

Kathrin

entry.title: Introducing the Financial Closing Assistant
entry.content:
Yesterday SAP introduced the Autonomous Enterprise and Autonomous Finance Financial Management Software Solutions | SAP.  Autonomous Finance will allow SAP and its partners to rethink every aspect of the finance department for the better.  SAP is introducing exciting new innovations across all areas of finance.  From Financial Planning & Analysis, to Revenue Management, to Treasury, Tax, and Compliance.  In short, SAP is introducing solutions for end-to-end autonomous finance and with it new agents and assistants which can be found on SAP.com like the Accounting Accruals Agent. 

Another new product is an Assistant.  An assistant is simply a collection of agents orchestrated to manage an end-to-end business process.  Think of them like a team that can take charge of higher order tasks in the enterprise.  In this blog the assistant that I am going to focus on is the new Financial Closing Assistant. 

The Financial Closing Assistant orchestrates end-to-end closing within SAP Cloud ERP by surfacing bottlenecks, automating journal postings and reconciliations, speeding accrual calculations, and resolving exceptions. For finance leaders, this helps reduce close cycle time, ensure on-time reports, and eliminate last-minute surprises. General availability planned for Q2 2026.

Financial Closing Assistant, Agent Status Cards, and Autonomous Finance

 

 

 

Finance and accounting professionals responsible for the timely and accurate financial closing of several company codes or more often face a mountain of repetitive, manual tasks that consume the first few days of every closing period, leaving little time for valuable analysis.

For many finance professionals, the close is a process in need of an overhaul.  There is a lot of work that must be done to get true visibility on the numbers, but it’s stressful, time-intensive and error-prone.  Few finance and accounting professionals are allowed to go on vacation during the close.  So, how does the Financial Closing Assistant make this process better?

The Financial Closing Assistant helps run your financial close end-to-end—flagging bottlenecks, automating the busywork, and fixing errors before they cause delays. It keeps the close moving so your team can focus on the work that matters.  The Assistant sweats the small stuff, so you don’t have to.

Agentic team status shown in SAP Advanced Financial Closing

From clearing, journal entries and intercompany reconciliation to accruals and final sign-off — dozens of steps, dozens of decisions. The SAP Financial Closing Assistant brings them all together.  Combined with SAP Advanced Financial Closing and the SAP accounting automation solutions by BlackLine, finance and accounting professionals now can proactively coordinate all activities of the financial close spanning system jobs, agent activities, and accounting professional activities.    

Additionally, Joule allows real-time conversations to take place.  For instance, with the Analytical Business Insights Agent, generating a dynamic chart, the booklet automatically adapts alongside the Joule conversation — highlighting the exact data being discussed.

The Analytical Business Insights Agent, showing two views of one conversations side-by-side

 

Two views. One conversation.  Again, Joule summarizes the financials and generates commentary on the spot — automatically captured in the booklet for seamless review.  This provides financial analysis at the speed of normal conversation and can give you a comprehensive picture of the financial health of your business — in minutes, not days.

 

 

The Analytical Business Insights Agent showing commentary

 

Another powerful agent is the Intercompany Reconciliation and Matching Agent. In the past, reconciling intercompany balances meant days of cross-referencing transactions across company codes, chasing discrepancies by email, and manually aligning records that never quite matched.

The Intercompany Reconciliation and Matching Agent

 

 

The agent automatically compares intercompany receivables and payables across all entity pairs, flagging items that require attention.  For each match, the agent shows its full reasoning — the originating documents on both sides — giving complete transparency into every decision. A quick review confirms accuracy. Avery approves. What used to take days of back-and-forth is resolved in minutes.  with agentic close, you get more accurate numbers, delivered faster.

BlackLine is a unique and longstanding Solution Extension partner dedicated to delivering with SAP exceptional customer value with the gold standard for Record to Report (R2R).  As SAP drives the vision for autonomous finance forward, SAP and BlackLine plan to extend this gold standard through coordinated AI capabilities, working towards a truly complementary autonomous close for our customers.

 

SAP accounting automation solutions by BlackLine along with SAP Advanced Financial Closing within Record to Report

 

The SAP and BlackLine partnership is dedicated to automating the manual, spreadsheet-heavy, and labor-intensive activities that finance and accounting teams face daily.  SAP accounting automation solutions by BlackLine align cohesively with the SAP autonomous enterprise, complementing the newly announced Financial Closing Assistant and SAP Advanced Financial Closing integration to elevate end-to-end accounting processes.

Through our ongoing commitment to collaborative innovation, SAP and BlackLine will continue to address both SAP and non-SAP data using SAP Business AI platform.  This powerful partnership allows customers the flexibility and scale required to deliver the maximum business impact.

SAP and BlackLine will continue to address both SAP and non-SAP data using the SAP Business AI platform

 

In summary, for many, the financial close means tight deadlines, manual data crunching, and high pressure. But now, with the SAP Financial Closing Assistant, accounting an finance professionals can enjoy a financial close that

  • Transforms from period-end to continuous close
  • Improves completeness, correctness, compliance and auditability
  • Keeps the human-in-the-loop
  • Easily provides financial performance insights
  • Is complementary with the SAP accounting automation solutions by BlackLine

The SAP Financial Closing Assistant, supercharged with Agentic AI, enables autonomous specialists running complex, data-intensive tasks in the background while the accounting professional focuses on decisions that matter.  For more information on the Financial Closing Assistant, please visit or visit sap.com and for more information on other assistants for finance please visit Joule Agents for Finance.

entry.title: Re: How to Create a Cumulative Sales Volume by Month in S...
entry.content:
Good day @Salma20 

Thank you for your question.

You can achieve your requirement in Public Cloud, but the best place to create the cumulative logic is not the Custom CDS View.

The reason is, a custom CDS View calculated field is suitable for row level expressions, not dynamic running totals across months. A Custom Analytical Query can aggregate monthly sales volume and can support calculated and restricted measures, but it is not the recommended layer for a flexible month by month running total. For a dynamic cumulative sales volume by month, I recommend you model the monthly sales volume in a Custom Analytical Query  

and consume it in a Story through Manage KPIs and Reports,  where running total calculations are supported.

I would recommend the following design:

  1. Use a released sales analytical CDS view or cube as the source.
  2. If needed, create a Custom CDS View only to enable you prepare the sales volume fields and month field.
  3. Create a Custom Analytical Query to expose monthly sales volume.
  4. Consume that query in a Story through Manage KPIs and Reports.
  5. Use running total calculation to create the cumulative sales volume calculation in the story layer.
  6. Validate that January equals January, February equals January plus February, and March equals January plus February and March.

That would ensure you keep the logic clean. You are allowed to create groups, KPIs, drilldowns, reports, and stories. The Public Cloud can also launch analytical cloud stories from the Fiori Launchpad.

2736239 - Calculated field return "0" while previewing data in Custom CDS View app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

2563022 - How to use functions in expression editor while editing calculated fields - SAP for Me

3702507 - Known Limitations and By-Design Behaviors of Calculation Features in SAP Analytics Cloud (Collective KBA) - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Unable to add discount percentage to Text items via M...
entry.content:
Hello,

The product-level discount (TypeCode 7PR6) is applied only when the Product node is present at the item level.

If the Item is created as a free-text item (i.e, no specific product is provided), the system does not evaluate product-based discounts unless the request explicitly contains a Product node.

To solve the issue you need to first create the External Customer Invoice Request in status To Be Invoiced (SettlementPriorityCode should not be passed see KBA 2389023). In a second call you can update the Discount value via the XML Payload. The system will then update the Discount value for the free-text item.

 

We also created the following KBA for this question: 3702376 - Discount Percentage is not Determined for a Text Item When Creating External Customer Invoice Request via the Web Service

 

Best regards,

Kathrin

entry.title: Custom notifications and widget events in SAP DM POD 2.0
entry.content:
We are developing custom widgets for SAP Digital Manufacturing Cloud POD 2.0 and require guidance on two related topics concerning notification triggering and communication between different widgets. Both questions relate to custom widget development within the POD framework. 

Triggering a custom notification from a production process and catching it in a custom POD 2.0 widget

We need to understand the correct approach for raising a custom notification from a production process and subscribing to and handling that notification inside a custom widget running in POD 2.0.

Specifically, we would like to know:

  • What is the recommended mechanism to trigger a custom notification from within a production process in SAP DM for POD 2.0?
  • How should a custom widget in POD 2.0 subscribe to and receive such a notification from the Production Process?
  • Is it possible to provide some documentation or an example case?


Publishing an event from one custom widget to trigger a function in another custom widget.

We have two custom widgets:  After a consumption is recorded in custom widget 1, we want to notify custom widget 2 so it can call a refresh function to reload its data.

We would like to know:

What is the correct way for a custom widget to publish a custom event within the POD 2.0 widget?

  • How should the receiving widget subscribe to that event and invoke a specific function (e.g., a refresh/reload method) in response?
  • Is there a lifecycle hook or best practice for registering and unregistering these subscriptions within POD 2.0 custom widgets?
  • Can you provide a minimal working example showing the publish/subscribe pattern between two custom widgets in POD 2.0?

entry.title: 🎉 Celebrating the Successful Launch of Joule in SAP for Me
entry.content:
The moment we’ve been working toward is here - and it’s one worth celebrating!

We are thrilled to announce the successful launch of Joule in SAP for Me, marking a major milestone in SAP’s journey to deliver a simpler, smarter, and more intuitive experience. This launch is more than a feature release - it is a transformation in how customers interact with SAP for Me.

 

 

A New Era for SAP for Me

SAP for Me has long been one of SAP’s most widely used customer portals. With the introduction of Joule, it now enters a new era.

Joule serves as a unified, AI-powered entry point into SAP for Me, fundamentally reshaping how customers navigate, engage, and get things done.

By leveraging conversational and agentic AI, Joule introduces a new way of working - one that prioritizes ease, speed, and personalization. This means users no longer have to search through complex menus or multiple touchpoints. Instead, they can simply ask, explore, and act.

 

What Joule Brings to SAP for Me

At its core, Joule is designed to understand business context and help users interact with systems using natural language.

Now embedded in SAP for Me, Joule delivers:

  • Effortless navigation – quickly find what you need without clicking through layers of pages
  • Advanced self-service – resolve tasks faster with guided, AI-driven support
  • Personalized assistance – tailored responses based on your role, context, and needs
  • Fast task execution – move from question to action in seconds, not minutes

In short, Joule transforms SAP for Me from a portal into a conversation-driven experience.

 

From Interface to Intelligence

This launch also reflects SAP’s broader vision for SAP Business AI: Joule is not just another chatbot - it is designed to connect data, processes, and insights across the enterprise, helping users work smarter and make better decisions faster.

Whether it’s retrieving information, navigating tools, or executing tasks, Joule brings intelligence directly into the flow of work. It reduces friction, accelerates outcomes, and enables users to focus on what truly matters.

And now, with its integration into SAP for Me, these capabilities are available right where customers need them most.

 

A Milestone Worth Celebrating

The successful launch of Joule in SAP for Me is the result of strong collaboration, innovation, and a shared commitment to improving the customer experience. It demonstrates how SAP continues to turn its AI strategy into real, tangible value - delivering solutions that are not only powerful but also practical and user-centric.

For our customers, this means:

  • Faster time to resolution
  • More intuitive interactions
  • A seamless and modern support experience

For SAP, it’s another step forward in making AI a natural part of everyday enterprise work.

 

What’s Next?

While this launch is a huge achievement, it’s also just the beginning.

As Joule continues to evolve, we can expect even more capabilities, deeper integrations, and expanded use cases across SAP’s portfolio. The vision is clear: a fully connected, AI-powered experience that supports users across every process and at every touchpoint.

Joule in SAP for Me will be rolled out in phases with the goal to make it available to all users by end of May 2026. In case it’s not visible yet for you, please stay tuned.

 

Where to find more information?

  • SAP for Me: https://me.sap.com/home
  • Support Webcasts
  • Joule AI Assistant

Let’s celebrate this success and continue shaping what’s next, together 🚀

 

Try Joule in SAP for Me and experience how AI is redefining your journey with SAP.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Systems Time Zone
entry.content:
Dear @Zlizardo,

In Public Cloud the system time zone cannot be changed to Madrid / Spain time. The cloud system time zone is kept in UTC by SAP as part of the standard cloud design. SAP explains that UTC is used as the main operating system and that the operating system time zone cannot be changed because this would interfere with system operations such as upgrades and maintenance

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3514300

For communication users and integrations the time zone cannot be changed also. SAP explicitly states that changing the time zone for a communication user in Public Cloud is not supported. Therefore, integration scenarios should continue to use UTC as the reference time, with any required local-time conversion handled outside S/4HANA for example in middleware or the receiving application

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2708067

Summary:

System time zone cannot be adjusted to Madrid / Spain time. System, technical and integration timestamps may remain in UTC by design

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Asymmetric Reporting Layout
entry.content:
What is Asymmetric Reporting?

It is our pleasure to announce the introduction of a new innovation in SAP Analytics Cloud, delivered as part of the 2026 Q2 QRC release.

Asymmetric Reporting introduces a new way to design tables in SAP Analytics Cloud, enabling greater flexibility in how data is structured and presented within a single table.

Instead of relying on a strictly uniform layout, where all rows and columns follow the same dimensions, hierarchies, and granularity, Asymmetric Reporting enables selected aspects of a table layout such as measures, time ranges, and visible hierarchy levels, to vary across different sections of the same table.

In practice, this means that a single table can combine multiple perspectives. For example, one part of the table may focus on actuals at a detailed monthly level, while another section presents forecasts or budgets at a higher level of aggregation. These views coexist within the same table, without requiring separate reporting objects or duplicated structures.

This capability helps users design more expressive and context-aware tables directly in SAP Analytics Cloud, better reflecting the way business questions are actually asked and answered.

 

Why it matters: Real-world reporting is not symmetric

Traditional table structures in BI and planning tools are typically symmetric by design. This means that the same dimensions, hierarchies, and filters are applied consistently across all rows and columns of a report.

While this approach ensures simplicity and consistency, it often does not reflect the reality of business reporting, especially in financial planning and analysis scenarios.

In practice, different parts of the same report frequently require different levels of detail or different analytical perspectives. For example:

  • Actuals are often analyzed at a monthly or even daily level of granularity
  • Forecasts may start from a specific cut-off date
  • Budgets are frequently reviewed at a quarterly or yearly level

Bringing all of these perspectives into a single symmetric table often leads to compromises: duplicated reports, complex workarounds, or loss of clarity in how data is presented.

From a planning perspective, this limitation becomes even more visible. An additional challenge comes from “unbooked” combinations that must remain visible for data entry and forecast collection, even when no transactional data exists yet. Business users want to compare actuals, forecasts, and budgets side by side, but each of these elements naturally follows a different logic and time structure. Forcing them into a single rigid model can reduce both usability and analytical accuracy.

Asymmetric Reporting in SAP Analytics Cloud addresses this challenge by allowing different parts of a table to reflect different structures and levels of detail, while still being part of a single, coherent model. This makes it possible to align table design more closely with real business processes, rather than adapting business logic to technical constraints.

 

Example and typical use cases

A typical example is a rolling forecast table.

In this scenario:

  • Accounts are displayed in the rows
  • Actuals are shown from January up to a selected cut-off date
  • Rest of Year Forecast starts after the cut-off date through the end of the period
  • Full Year Actuals plus Forecast provides a consolidated yearly view
  • Full Year Budget is displayed at a quarterly level
  • A Delta highlights the difference between Budget and Actuals plus Forecast

Each column represents a different perspective, with its own logic and level of detail. This is what makes the table asymmetric.

This approach is especially useful for:

  • Rolling forecasts
  • Budget vs Actual vs Forecast analysis
  • Financial statements such as P&L or Balance Sheet
  • Management reporting dashboards

 

See it in action

To see how this comes together in practice, watch the following demo:

 

In this example, notice how:

  • Time ranges adjust dynamically
  • Different measures display different levels of granularity
  • The table adapts based on user input

 

How it works: The building blocks behind Asymmetric Reporting

Asymmetric Reporting is enabled by a combination of three key innovations that work together.

First, Measure Input Control in Restricted Measures and Cross Calculations allows input controls, such as date, version, or measure selection, to dynamically drive calculations within the table.

Second, Dynamic Time Filters for “Rest of Period” automatically adjust time ranges based on the current context, enabling scenarios like “Actuals up to a selected date” and “Forecast for the remaining period.”

Third, Visibility Filters for Table Widgets control which dimension members or hierarchy levels are displayed for each measure, allowing different parts of the table to present different views.

Combined, these capabilities enable each column or row to behave independently while remaining part of a single, consistent table.

With these building blocks, you can:

  • Combine multiple reporting perspectives in a single table
  • Mix different levels of granularity (monthly, quarterly, yearly)
  • Dynamically adapt time ranges and calculations
  • Reduce the need for multiple tables or manual workarounds
  • Create reports that better reflect real business logic

 

Share your feedback

Asymmetric Reporting introduces a more flexible way to design tables in SAP Analytics Cloud.

By combining dynamic calculations, adaptive time filtering, and flexible display control, it enables reporting structures that better align with real-world business needs. This results in more intuitive reports, improved analysis, and a more efficient BI and Planning experience.

That being said, we now want to hear from you. Your feedback directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please let us know your thoughts. Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

Hello,
In the below SQL in the 6th line the 'b' (CSKT) has been specified in the left postion but whereas in all the rest (5,7 and 8 of the lines the 'b' has been specified in right position, so in this case pls. let us know wheter this SQL is ok? is it pulls correct data? is it pulls ALL the qualfied records? SELECT a~kokrs a~bukrs a~kostl b~ltext
  INTO TABLE (lt_cost_data)
  FROM csks AS a
  LEFT JOIN cskt AS b
  ON  a~kokrs EQ b~kokrs
  AND b~spras EQ sy-langu "Here I took 'B'(CSKT) in left position, is it ok?

entry.title: The Autonomous Enterprise and Intelligent Feedback Ecosystem—Powered by Your Feedback
entry.content:
Do you recognize the footpaths branching off the walkway in this photo?

Urban planners call these “desire paths”, and see them as valuable data, not defiance. Many start with desire paths, then build walkways where people have shown they are needed.

Desire paths echo the first of three important trends in our industry. It’s how we—SAP and our customers—are shaping future SAP products together. Here is a look into these three important trends:

1. SAP Customers and Users are becoming co‑creators through feedback, intent and intelligence

Just as human needs shape paths through a landscape—AI-supported products increasingly allow customers to shape their workflows through product feedback, expressed intent and configuration more than traditional feature requests.

Users convey desired workflow and outcomes and systems adapt (“I need this process in a different order, so that I can start here and end up with this output and that outcome.”). This transforms requestors into co-creators. Product boundaries ultimately become fluid, dynamically composed by AI agents.

2. Continuous in‑context feedback

While customer councils and other high-touch engagements are tremendously valuable, continuous in‑context feedback collection—like Joule chat and our PX In-Product Surveys—add low-touch sentiment and urgency signals, giving us depth where needed and scale where possible. Our transformation is built on this foundation of over 12 million in-product feedback records collected since 2020, including 4.4 million in 2025 alone.

Adding multimodal signals like usage telemetry and user behavior analytics helps build a holistic view of users’ desire paths.

3. AI‑augmented insight turns noise into decision‑grade signals

As feedback volumes and data sets continue to grow, AI-powered analytics tools help us see patterns that matter, where to investigate further, and where to act. Our AI platform helps us cluster and triangulate feedback at scale, with telemetry and user behavior analytics validating feedback topics against observed user behavior.

Market-driven, customer-centric roadmaps are impacted by our Continuous Influence services. Innovation flows from real user and customer feedback, not boardroom assumptions—breaking new ground and removing obstacles along the way.

These inputs, other crucial market signals, and the data relationships they reveal through analytics, help us prioritize actions and investments based on strategic goals, context and maximizing customer value.


Our vision: An Intelligent Feedback Ecosystem

We are harmonizing the most important feedback and telemetry data channels to reduce noise and maximize insights as we transform the development lifecycle into an innovation flywheel that moves the Autonomous Enterprise.

AI-powered analytics are central to this transformation—finding insights in sentiment, intent and behavior for actions that maximize the value of customer investments.

Product teams balance feedback, telemetry, other market signals, strategy, and product standards. Not all feedback is actionable immediately, but closing the loop with customers is vital to an intelligent feedback ecosystem.

Knowing where your feedback went, and the impact it made, grows trust and commitment to keep sharing feedback.

So please keep sharing your feedback and engage in SAP's Customer Influence services. Encourage users to leave in-product feedback. These are all vital steps on the path to future enterprise experiences.


Join us at SAP Sapphire in Madrid!

We would love to hear your comments and questions below—and to discuss this shared future in person
during our Strategy Talk —  Feedback to Delivery: How Customer Feedback Shapes SAP Products
and at our Demo Station at SAP Sapphire Madrid!
